---
title: Minimalist Grammars
bibliography: ../../references.bib
---

The formalisms we've seen so far—[TAGs](tree-adjoining-grammars.qmd), [type-logical grammars](type-logical-grammars.qmd), [CCGs](combinatory-categorial-grammars.qmd), and [MCFGs](mcfgs-and-lcfrs.qmd)—were developed primarily from computational and mathematical perspectives. Minimalist Grammars (MGs), introduced by @stabler1997derivational, come from a different direction entirely: they are a formalization of the core operations proposed in Chomsky's Minimalist Program, the dominant framework in theoretical syntax since the mid-1990s.

What makes MGs remarkable for our purposes is not the linguistic theory behind them but a formal result: @michaelis2001transforming showed that the string languages generated by MGs are exactly the same as those generated by MCFGs (and therefore TAGs and CCGs). A formalism motivated by linguistic theory converges on the same class of languages as formalisms motivated by computational considerations. This is perhaps the strongest evidence that the [MCS boundary](index.qmd) is a real property of natural language, not an artifact of any particular approach to grammar.

## The two operations

An MG has just two structure-building operations:

**Merge** combines two expressions. One expression (the *selector*) takes the other (the *selectee*) as an argument. This is analogous to function application in [CCG](combinatory-categorial-grammars.qmd) or to substitution in [TAGs](tree-adjoining-grammars.qmd).

**Move** displaces a subexpression from one position to another. This is what handles phenomena like wh-movement (*what did you see __?*), where the wh-phrase *what* appears at the front of the sentence but is interpreted as the object of *see*. Move is analogous to adjunction in TAGs—it's the operation that goes beyond context-free power.

## Formal definition

An MG is a tuple $G = \langle \Sigma, \text{sel}, \text{lic}, \text{Lex} \rangle$ where:

- $\Sigma$ is the terminal alphabet (the words)
- $\text{sel}$ is a finite set of *selectional features* (like syntactic categories: $v$, $d$, $t$, etc.)
- $\text{lic}$ is a finite set of *licensing features* (triggers for movement: $\text{wh}$, $\text{case}$, etc.)
- $\text{Lex}$ is a finite set of *lexical items*, where each lexical item is a string from $\Sigma^*$ paired with a sequence of features drawn from $\text{sel} \cup \overline{\text{sel}} \cup \text{lic} \cup \overline{\text{lic}}$

Features come in pairs: a *positive* feature (e.g. $=d$, meaning "select a $d$") and a corresponding *negative* feature (e.g. $d$, meaning "I am a $d$"). Merge is triggered when a positive selectional feature $=x$ on one expression matches a negative selectional feature $x$ on another. Move is triggered when a positive licensing feature $+y$ matches a negative licensing feature $-y$ somewhere in the same expression.

For example, a transitive verb like *sees* might be the lexical item:

$$\text{sees} :: =d\;=d\;v$$

This means: "I select two $d$-expressions (two DPs) and then I am a $v$ (a verb phrase)." A determiner like *the* might be:

$$\text{the} :: =n\;d\;{-\text{case}}$$

This means: "I select an $n$ (noun), become a $d$ (DP), and carry an unchecked case feature." When a verb's $=d$ feature matches the DP's $d$ feature via Merge, the DP becomes the verb's argument. Later, a functional head with $+\text{case}$ can trigger Move to check the DP's $-\text{case}$ feature, potentially displacing it.

## MG = MCFG

The key result, due to @michaelis2001transforming, is that MGs generate exactly the MCS languages. The proof works by showing that every MG can be converted to an equivalent MCFG and vice versa. The fan-out of the corresponding MCFG is bounded by the number of movement steps the MG allows.

This equivalence has practical implications: the [MCFG parsing algorithms](mcfgs-and-lcfrs.qmd) we discussed—and the one you'll implement in the [final project](../../assignments/final-project.ipynb)—can be used to parse MGs. @kobele2007parsing developed this connection, showing how to compile MGs into MCFGs and then parse the resulting MCFGs using standard techniques.

## Copying and beyond context-freeness

One way to understand why MGs (and MCFGs) go beyond context-free power is through the lens of *copying*. As Kobele discusses, many syntactic constructions involve a relationship between two positions in a sentence—a "filler" and a "gap"—where the same semantic content appears in both positions but is phonologically realized in only one. Wh-movement is the canonical example: in *what did you see __?*, *what* is the filler and the gap (marked __) is the position where the object is interpreted.

In a CFG, you cannot enforce a dependency between two arbitrarily separated positions—this is the same limitation that prevents CFGs from generating $\{ww \mid w \in \Sigma^+\}$, which we saw in the [pumping lemma section](../../phonological-patterns/generative-capacity/pumping-lemma.qmd). Move in an MG (and the corresponding multi-component mechanism in MCFGs and adjunction in TAGs) is what provides exactly the right amount of copying power to handle these dependencies without exceeding the MCS boundary.

## Connections to the subregular hierarchy

@graf2013local showed that the constraints on MG derivations—which movements are allowed, how features are checked—can often be characterized in terms of the [subregular hierarchy](../../phonological-patterns/subregular-hierarchy/index.qmd) that we developed in the phonological module. Just as phonological constraints on strings tend to be Strictly Local or Tier-based Strictly Local, syntactic constraints on derivation trees tend to fall into analogous subregular classes defined over trees rather than strings. This is a active area of research that connects the computational complexity of phonology and syntax within a single framework.

## The big picture

We've now seen four formalisms—[TAGs](tree-adjoining-grammars.qmd), [CCGs](combinatory-categorial-grammars.qmd), [MCFGs](mcfgs-and-lcfrs.qmd), and MGs—that capture the mildly context-sensitive languages. They come from different traditions and look very different on the surface:

| Formalism | Primitive objects | Key operations | Origin |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|--------|
| TAG | Elementary trees | Substitution, adjunction | Computational linguistics [@joshi1975tree] |
| CCG | Lexical categories | Application, composition, type-raising | Logic/philosophy [@steedman2000syntactic] |
| MCFG | Multi-component rules | String-tuple concatenation | Formal language theory [@seki1991multiple] |
| MG | Feature-bearing lexical items | Merge, Move | Generative grammar [@stabler1997derivational] |

Yet they converge on the same class of languages—and this is the class where natural language appears to live. The [final project](../../assignments/final-project.ipynb) asks you to implement a parser for MCFGs, which by the equivalence results, also serves as a parser for the languages generated by TAGs, CCGs, and MGs.
